content:
# Acts Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 246-247 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_017_page_001.jpg, ACT_017_page_002.jpg

---

**[1]** They traveled through Amphipolis and Apollonia and came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue. **[2]** As was his habit, Paul went in to join them, and over three Sabbaths he reasoned with them from the Scriptures, **[3]** opening them up and laying out the case that the Messiah had to suffer and rise from the dead. "This Jesus I am proclaiming to you," he said, "he is the Messiah." **[4]** Some of them were persuaded and threw in their lot with Paul and Silas — a large number of God-fearing Greeks and quite a few prominent women among them.

**[5]** But the Jewish leaders were consumed with jealousy. They recruited some thugs from the marketplace, formed a mob, and threw the city into chaos. They stormed Jason's house, hunting for Paul and Silas to drag them before the people's assembly. **[6]** When they could not find them, they hauled Jason and some of the other believers before the city officials, shouting, "These men who have turned the whole world upside down have come here too, **[7]** and Jason has welcomed them into his home! Every one of them defies the decrees of Caesar, claiming there is another king — one called Jesus." **[8]** The crowd and the city officials were deeply alarmed when they heard this. **[9]** They made Jason and the others post bond and then released them.

**[10]** That very night the believers sent Paul and Silas away to Berea. When they arrived, they went to the Jewish synagogue. **[11]** Now the Bereans were more open-minded than those in Thessalonica. They received the message with real eagerness and examined the Scriptures every day to see whether what Paul said was true. **[12]** Many of them believed, along with a considerable number of prominent Greek women and men. **[13]** But when the Jews in Thessalonica learned that Paul was preaching the word of God in Berea too, they came there as well, agitating and stirring up the crowds. **[14]** The believers immediately sent Paul off toward the coast, while Silas and Timothy stayed behind. **[15]** Paul's escorts brought him all the way to Athens, then headed back with instructions for Silas and Timothy to join him as soon as possible.

**[16]** While Paul waited for them in Athens, his spirit was deeply provoked when he saw that the city was saturated with idols. **[17]** So he debated in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing Greeks, and every day in the marketplace with whoever happened to be there. **[18]** Some Epicurean and Stoic philosophers began sparring with him. Some said, "What is this seed-picker trying to say?" Others said, "He seems to be a promoter of foreign gods" — because he was proclaiming Jesus and the resurrection. **[19]** They took hold of him and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, "May we know what this new teaching is that you are presenting? **[20]** You are bringing some strange ideas to our ears, and we want to know what they mean." **[21]** (All the Athenians and the foreigners living there spent their time doing nothing but discussing or listening to the latest ideas.)

**[22]** Paul stood up in the middle of the Areopagus and said: "People of Athens, I can see that in every way you are extremely religious. **[23]** For as I walked around and looked carefully at your objects of worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. What you worship without knowing — this is what I am declaring to you.

**[24]** "The God who made the world and everything in it — he is Lord of heaven and earth and does not live in temples built by human hands. **[25]** He is not served by human hands as though he needed anything, since he himself gives everyone life and breath and everything else. **[26]** From one man he made every nation of the human race to inhabit the whole face of the earth, having determined their appointed times and the boundaries of where they would live, **[27]** so that they would seek God, and perhaps grope for him and find him — though in fact he is not far from any one of us. **[28]** For in him we live and move and have our being, as even some of your own poets have said: 'For we are indeed his offspring.'

**[29]** "Since, then, we are God's offspring, we should not think that the divine nature is anything like gold or silver or stone — an image shaped by human craft and imagination. **[30]** In the past God overlooked such ignorance, but now he commands all people everywhere to repent, **[31]** because he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice through a man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead."

**[32]** When they heard about the resurrection of the dead, some of them sneered, but others said, "We want to hear you again on this subject." **[33]** At that, Paul left the council. **[34]** But some people joined him and believed. Among them were Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**On the translation of Acts 17:**

This chapter is one of the great dramatic arcs in Acts — from mob violence in Thessalonica, to intellectual honesty in Berea, to the supreme rhetorical moment at the Areopagus. The dynamic rendering aims to preserve the escalating momentum across all three scenes.

**Verse 5 — "thugs from the marketplace" (agoraioi andres):** Sinaiticus reads the phrase describing these men recruited from the agora. The Greek carries the connotation of idle loafers, street roughs — men available for hire in the marketplace. "Thugs" captures the menace and social class implied; a formal rendering like "wicked men" loses the street-level edge.

**Verse 6 — "turned the whole world upside down" (ten oikoumenen anastatosantes):** This is one of the most vivid accusations in the New Testament. The Greek anastatoo means to upset, destabilize, cause an upheaval. The English phrase "turned the world upside down" has become proverbial precisely because of this passage, and I have preserved it as a dynamic equivalent that carries the same force of alarm the city officials would have felt.

**Verse 11 — "more open-minded" (eugenesteroi):** Sinaiticus reads eugenesteroi, literally "more noble-born." But Luke's point is not about social class; it is about intellectual disposition — they received the word with eagerness and tested it against Scripture. "Open-minded" captures the actual meaning Luke intends: a nobility of character expressed through honest inquiry rather than knee-jerk hostility.

**Verse 18 — "seed-picker" (spermologos):** This Athenian slang term literally means "one who picks up seeds" — like a bird pecking at scraps. It was used contemptuously for someone who collected scraps of learning without real understanding. I have retained "seed-picker" as it is vivid and strange enough to convey the dismissive Athenian wit. A paraphrase like "babbler" loses the specific color of the insult.

**Verse 22 — "extremely religious" (deisidaimonesterous):** This word is deliberately ambiguous in Greek — it can mean "very religious" or "very superstitious." Paul is being diplomatically pointed. The rendering "extremely religious" preserves the surface compliment while allowing the subtext to emerge.

**Verse 23 — "TO AN UNKNOWN GOD" (AGNOSTW THEW):** Sinaiticus reads agnostw thew. Paul's rhetorical pivot here is extraordinary — he transforms Athenian religious caution (hedging their bets with an altar to a god they might have missed) into an opening for the gospel. The dynamic rendering preserves Paul's audacious interpretive move: "What you worship without knowing — this is what I am declaring to you."

**Verse 27 — "grope for him" (pselaphao):** This verb means to feel around for something in the dark, to grope or fumble. It is a powerful image of humanity reaching out for God with uncertain hands. I have kept "grope" as it preserves the physicality and uncertainty of the Greek.

**Verse 28 — "For we are indeed his offspring":** Paul quotes from Aratus's Phaenomena (and possibly Cleanthes' Hymn to Zeus). This is a masterclass in cross-cultural communication — Paul meets the Athenians on their own intellectual ground before redirecting them toward the God who actually raised a man from the dead.

**Verse 32 — the Athenian split:** The chapter ends with the characteristic Athenian response: some mock, some postpone, a few believe. Luke does not editorialize. The resurrection — anastasis nekron — was the breaking point. For Greek philosophy, the body was a prison; resurrection was not good news but an absurdity. Paul's willingness to press this claim in the heart of Greek intellectual culture is one of the boldest moments in Acts.

— *PNEUMA*
